coredata.dc:title: A coumarin-connected carboxylic indolinium sensor for cyanide detection in absolute aqueous medium and its application in biological cell imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Cheng, Siyao
Pan, Xihao
Shi, Mingyang
Su, Ting
Zhang, Cheng
Zhao, Wei
Dong, Wei
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent sensor has been noticed in detecting system due to its sensitive, selective, operational simplicity and low cost. We designed a coumarin-connected carboxylic indolium sensor molecule that is water-soluble and cytomembrane-permeable. This infrared (IR) emitter is selectively sensitive towards cyanide detection in aqueous media according to CN− nucleophilic attack on the indole C=N function. Upon the addition of CN− anion, the color of sensor in water varied from blue to colorless by naked eyes and fluorescence quenching was observed by spectroscopic method. This was because the intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) effect occurred when the fluorescent sensor was added with CN−. The minimum detection limit of the sensor's fluorescence response to CN− is 4.44 × 10−7 mol/L. Furthermore, the cytotoxicity test shows the sensor has lower cytotoxicity, and indicates that this sensor can be utilized for practical detection of trace cyanide in wastewater.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent sensor
Water-soluble
Cyanide
Nucleophilic attack
Cell imaging

Anion sensing properties in absolutely aqueous solution
The spectral response of sensor CID to CN−-titration
Detection of CN− in biological cells

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–vis absorption spectra of CID (1.0 × 10−5 M) with various analytes in H2O [0.01 M Tris-HCl buffer, pH 7.2] in the presence of CN−, F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, Ac−, HSO4 2−, H2PO4 3−, ClO4 −, N3 −and SCN−. Inset: Color change. (b) Fluorescence spectra of CID (1.0 × 10−5 M) with various analytes in H2O. (λex = 500 nm, slit: 5 nm/10 nm). Inset: Color change.

(a) UV–vis spectra of CID (1.0 × 10−5 M) in H2O [0.01 M Tris-HCl buffer, pH 7.2] upon adding an increasing concentration of CN−. Inset: Color change. (b) Fluorescence spectra of CID upon adding an increasing concentration of CN−. Inset: Color change. (c) Changes of absorbance intensity at 584 nm with the addition of CN−(0.1 M). Inset: the good linearity in titration CN− 0–8
μL
(R2 = 0.97597). (d) Changes of fluorescence intensity at 642 nm with the addition of CN−(0.1 M); insert: the good linearity in titration CN− 0–8
μL
(R2 = 0.98177).

(a) UV–vis spectra of sensor CID at 584 nm with addition of CN−in the presence of 50 equiv. of other anions in H2O. (b) Fluorescence of CID at 642 nm with addition of CN− in the presence of 50 equiv. of other anions in H2O.
1HNMR spectra of sensor CID in DMSO‑d 6 before and after the addition of CN−.
The main contribution of the frontier molecular orbitals of CID and CID-CN.

(a) Cell cytotoxicity effect of CID on B10–F10 cells. 1, contrast; 2, 0.01 μM; 3, 0.1 μM; 4, 1 μM; 5, 10 μM; 6, 50 μM. Data are expressed as mean values ± standard error of the mean of three independent experiments, each performed in three triplicates.

Fluorescence microscopy images of B16–F10 cells with CID (a, b, c); Fluorescence microscopy images of B16–F10 cells with CID and CN −
(d, e, f). a, d shows the corresponding brightfield image. (red channel, λex = 488 nm, λem = 580–630 nm).

head.ce:title: A coumarin-connected carboxylic indolinium sensor for cyanide detection in absolute aqueous medium and its application in biological cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, Nanjing, 210094, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nanjing University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, Nanjing, 210094, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent sensor has been noticed in detecting system due to its sensitive, selective, operational simplicity and low cost. We designed a coumarin-connected carboxylic indolium sensor molecule that is water-soluble and cytomembrane-permeable. This infrared (IR) emitter is selectively sensitive towards cyanide detection in aqueous media according to CN− nucleophilic attack on the indole C=N function. Upon the addition of CN− anion, the color of sensor in water varied from blue to colorless by naked eyes and fluorescence quenching was observed by spectroscopic method. This was because the intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) effect occurred when the fluorescent sensor was added with CN−. The minimum detection limit of the sensor's fluorescence response to CN− is 4.44 × 10−7 mol/L. Furthermore, the cytotoxicity test shows the sensor has lower cytotoxicity, and indicates that this sensor can be utilized for practical detection of trace cyanide in wastewater.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of industry, all kind of industrial pollution has been produced. Various contaminants were released directly into rivers and lakes without any further treatment, which caused dystrophication and soil contamination. Furthermore, long-term accumulation of hazardous substances could lead to serious health problems [1–5]. As we all know, cyanide is terribly hazardous material to human tissue at very low concentration that destroys vascular, visual and metabolic systems. Nevertheless, cyanides are widespread in various important industrial applications including gold mining, electroplating, tanning and metallurgy [6–8]. It is very urgent to get rid of the poisonous cyanide in waste water, and determination of cyanide in wastewater samples is a key factor. Therefore, it is great of important to develop effective techniques for the determination of cyanide in drinking water [9].

Up to now, some options are used for detecting CN− such as titrimetric, electrochemistry and spectroscopy [10–13]. They are sensitive and selective, but tedious operation, insufficient detection limit and not real time. Recently, fluorescent sensor has been noticed for CN− ions detection, because it's specificity, sensitivity, operational simplicity and low cost [14–25]. Its detection mechanism contains coordination mechanism, hydrogen bonding interaction, supramolecular self-assembly, nucleophilic addition reaction. However, the majority of fluorescent sensors have some drawbacks, for example, poor solubility in water, low selectivity under from coexisting anions interference, poor application in the “naked eye” detection [26–35]. As well as lots of fluorescent probe's emission signals can be interfered by the cell autofluorescence, so they cannot be used in cell imaging well. Therefore, it is of vital importance to design one that the fluorescent sensor's emission signals discrete from the autofluorescence of biomolecules (195–600 nm) in biological window [36–40]. It has been confirmed that the coumarin molecule can offer enough charge transfer in the coumarin-connected molecule to promote those sensors' absorption and emission in the red region so as to effectively eliminate cell autofluorescence [40–42].

In this work, we have designed and synthesized a fluorescent coumarin - indole probe CID (as shown in Scheme 1 ) as a colorimetric and turn-off fluorescent probe for detecting CN− in absolute aqueous [43–49]. The Carboxylate group was tethered to indole moiety for improving water solubility. Carboxylated indole unit as an electron withdrawing group and 7-(Diethylamino) coumarin-3-carbaldehyde as an electron donating group were covalently linked through a double bond (D-π-A). The color of sensor CID is observed to change from blue to colorless by naked eye due to quenching of the fluorescence because ICT mechanism is disrupted in presence of CN−. The spectroscopic experiments have shown that the probe CID had a fast kinetics, high sensitivity, good selectivity and low detection limit (4.44 × 10−7 mol/L) for selective recognition of CN− in 100% aqueous solution. Also, the synthesized IR emitting sensor (CID) is found to possess lower cytotoxicity, cytomemebrane permeability and may be useful for detection of cyanide in biological cells by fluorescence imaging in the spectral region 580 nm to 630 nm, where autofluorescence of living cells is low. The binding mechanism of CID-CN was further confirmed by the 1H NMR, MS analyses and DFT optimized structures of CID and CID-CN adduct.

All reagents and solvents were purchased from commercial sources without further purification. The deionized water was used throughout the experiment. The 1HNMR and 13CNMR spectra were taken on 500 MHz and 126 MHz respectively, using DMSO as solvent and tetramethylsilane as an internal standard. UV–vis spectras were detected on a Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrometer. Fluorescent spectras were recorded at a Shimadzu RF-5301 fluorescence spectrophotometer. pH values were achieved by a Sartorius (PB-10) digital pH meter (LeiCi, Shanghai, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of sensor CID
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis route of CID is shown in Scheme 1 . The compound (1) was prepared according to literature [50]. Then compound (1) (0.232 g, 1 mmol) and (2) (0.245 g, 1 mmol) were dissolved in ethyl alcohol (20 mL), stirred and refluxed at 76 °C for 12 h when the reaction is finished, we evaporated the solvent and purified by recrystallization from Hexane-EtOH to get CID as a dark green powder.
MS m/z [M]+ Calcd for C28H31N2O4 + 459.2299, found 459.2278.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General procedures for UV–vis and fluorescence spectrum experiments
ce:section.ce:para: All spectrum experiments were conducted in 100% aqueous solution. Most of the anions (F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, AcO−, H2PO4 −, HSO4 −and ClO4 −) were prepared from their tetrabutylammonium salt, CN−, N3 − and SCN− were from their sodium salts. Any change in the spectroscopy of the sensor CID with different anions will be recorded and the concentration of the bulk solution will be maintained at 10 μM in all experiments.

The fluorescence quantum yield of CID was calculated by the following equation.
Φx = Φst (Dx/Dst) (Ast/Ax) (ηx 2/ηst 2)
Where ‘st’ subscript represents the reference and the Φst as the quantum yield of the standard. ‘x’ stands for the sample. A is the absorbance at the excitation wavelength, η is the refractive index of the solvent used, D is the area under the emission optical spectrum. Rohidamine B (Φ = 0.89 in water) at pH 7.4 as the criterion.

B16–F10 cells were cultured in RPMI-1640 supplemented with 10% FBS (Fetal Bovine Serum), 100 μg/mL penicillin and 100 μg/mL streptomycin at 37 °C in an atmosphere of 5% CO2 and 95% air for 24 h. Before the experiment, the cells well placed in 6-well plate, followed by adding the sensor CID (10 μM). The cells were incubated at 37 °C in environment of 5% CO2 and 95% air for 3 h, and then washed three of the holes with PBS for three times. Subsequently, 20 μM CN− were injected into the three holes and incubated for 20 min to enter recipient cell. Subsequently, the cells contained sensor and CN− were washed by PBS buffer for three times to eliminate floating colors. Then the detection results were verified by taking fluorescence images with the red channel of the fluorescence microscope (λem = 570–620 nm).

In the design process, we are committed to develop a sensor with water-soluble, cytomembrane-permeable, IR emitting, and sensitive detection of CN− in order to specifically bind CN- in 100% aqueous solution and detect the content of CN− in biological and environmental systems. The synthesis of CID by aldol condensation mechanism were outlined in Scheme 1. In order to detect the sensitivity and selectivity of CID toward CN−, a series of spectral experiments about CID were carried out in the present of different anions (F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, AcO−, H2PO4 −, HSO4 −, ClO4 −, CN−, N3 −and SCN−). With the addition of CN−, spectral experiments showed that CID could measure the concentration of CN−. We also explored the performance of the sensor CID in detecting CN− in biological cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anion sensing properties in absolutely aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: In order to study the ability of specific recognition of sensors CID, we conducted experiments with UV–vis spectrophotometry and fluorescence spectrometer. The maximum absorption bond of sensor CID was at 584 nm in H2O (0.01 M Tris-HCl buffer, pH 7.2). From Fig. 2a, it showed that a significant absorbance decrease was observed in presence of CN− and the other anions, including F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, AcO−, HSO4 −, H2PO4 −, ClO4 −, N3 − and SCN− (50 equiv). With the addition of CN− to CID solution, the absorption peak at 584 nm dramatically decreased and a new peak suddenly appeared at 425 nm. In the fluorescence spectrum, the sensor CID had strongly fluorescence in red region (λem max = 642 nm). Only addition of CN−, the fluorescence emission was at 642 nm turn-off in Fig. 1 b. From Fig. 1a insert and Fig. 1b insert, it observed that the color of solution varied from blue to colorless with naked eye and the color of solution varied from red to yellow under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation after upon addition of CN−. Under the same conditions, other anions had no significant effect on UV–vis spectra and fluorescence spectra so that the sensor CID had a promising application for selectively detecting CN−.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The spectral response of sensor CID to CN−-titration
ce:section.ce:para:
The UV–vis titration and fluorophotometric titration of sensor CID toward CN− (0.1 M) were carried out in aqueous solution. When the concentration of CN− increased in the bulk solution contained the CID (10 μM), an obvious reduction of the UV–vis absorption peak at 584 nm and a significant increasing of absorbance at 425 nm occurred (Fig. 2 a). Moreover, the calculated limit of detection for the UV–vis for sensing CN− according to the equation 3σB/S was estimated to be 3.35 × 10−7 mol/L, which was far below the World Health Organization (WHO) permissive level for cyanide concentration 1.9
μ
mol/L in drinking water. After 1.0 eq CN− was added, the fluorescent emission intensity a t 642 nm rapidly weakened until the fluorescence of sensor CID was quenched. From Fig. 2c–d, it showed that the absorbance and fluorescence intensity both appeared huge mutation at a function of [CN−]/[μL]. In Fig. 2c insert, the absorbance of CID displayed good linearity with CN−-titration with the linear regression coefficient of 0.97597. Besides, Fig. 2d insert indicated that the fluorescence intensity of CID and the function [CN−]/[μL] was a perfect linear relation (R2 = 0.98177). The fluorescence quantum yield of CID was calculated (Φx = 0.151). Therefore, the results indicated that CID was a promising fluorescent sensor for quantitative determination of CN− in absolutely aqueous solution.

In order to prove the selectivity response of CID, but no response to other anions including F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, AcO−, H2PO4 −, HSO4 −, ClO4 −, CN−, N3 − and SCN−. The contrast experiments were performed with 50 equiv. of CN− and 50 equiv. of other anions in 100% water solution [51,52]. From Fig. 3 a, b, their absorbance and fluorescence intensity were informed that the anti-interference ability of CID to CN− in the presence of other commonly competitive anions was outstanding and the CID could apply in complicated waste water for detecting CN−.

To get insight into the plausible binding mechanism between CID and CN− by 1HNMR-titration experiments and MS methods. Sensor CID and tetrabutylammonium cyanide were dissolved in DMSO‑d 6 respectively, and then tetrabutylammonium cyanide solution was added into CID solution with dropwise. As shown in Fig. 4 , all 1H NMR signals were redshifted upon addition of CN− and the corresponding OH signal disappeared at 13.30. The vinylic protons (Ha and Hb) presented up-field shift from 7.96 ppm, 7.61 ppm to 7.68 ppm, 7.48 ppm due to breaking of the conjugation. In addition, the chemical shifts of the N-methyl protons (Hc) shifted from 4.49 ppm to 4.20 ppm. The binding mechanism of CID-CN was further confirmed by the MS analyses, where a peak at 486.23 m/z was occurred. These observations exposed the formation of stable anionic species CID-CN in this process.

In order to complete the mechanism of CID upon binding with CN−, we used the Gaussian 09 program, B3LYP/6-31G(d) method. The density functional theory (DFT) optimized structures of CID and CID-CN have been displayed in Fig. 5 . The HOMO orbitals of CID were homogeneous diffused on coumarin unit and indolium vinylene unit due to integrated π-conjugation and active ICT processes. After the addition of CN-, the π-conjugate state of CID is broken due to the transfer of electron density from anion to the receptor CID and ICT process is blocked, showing that the electron clouds of CID–CN– are localization on coumarin moiety and the band gap of CID-CN- (3.4797 eV) obviously surpassed the band gap of CID (2.4704 eV). The theoretical results well explained the red shift phenomenon in UV–vis. Thus, we can conclude that the CID detected CN− by a nucleophilic addition reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of CN− in biological cells
ce:section.ce:para:
To explore the potential biological application of sensor CID, we tested the biocompatibility of the CID against B16–F10 cells in vitro. MTT assay is an important evaluation criterion for biological systems. As shown in Fig. 6
, the cell viability was reached above 85%, even though the concentration of sensor CID was 50 μM. Therefore, the sensor CID had a possibility for applying in detecting CN− in biological cells. It can be seen in Fig. 7 that sensor CID could enter cells and widely spread in the whole cytoplasmic matrix. When cells were incubated for 30 min at 37 °C in the presence of CID (10 μM), and then it showed vividness red fluorescence. In contrast, there only presented a large black area when CID has been pre-processed by CN−. These results demonstrate that sensor CID could be used for CN− detection in biological cells.

In conclusion, we have successfully developed a water-soluble, cytomembrane-permeable, NIR emitting Indole-Based coumarin fluorescent sensor CID based on coumarin-connected carboxylic indolium. The sensor shows noteworthy color change from blue to colorless with the addition of CN−. The desirable LOD of sensor on fluorescence response towards CN− is 4.44 × 10−7 mol/L, and the sensor has good anti-interference ability to CN− over other anions in water. Furthermore, the cytotoxicity test shows the CID has lower cytotoxicity. Due to the sensor CID outstanding performances, the sensor CID has a great application prospect in detecting CN− in biological systems and environment systems.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we do not have any commercial or associative interest that represents a conflict of interest in connection with the work submitted.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the financial support by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) (grant number 51573078).
